reports.title: Oil Flash Note: Simple math
reports.headline: JPM argues the oil market is clearing via forced demand loss and heavy inventory draws after a Hormuz-related supply shock, with further price upside likely needed to balance the market.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan frames this as a macro commodities flash note rather than single-stock research, arguing that a roughly 14 mbd supply disruption tied to the Strait of Hormuz closure has overwhelmed normal balancing mechanisms because Gulf spare capacity is effectively inaccessible. The note says the market has instead adjusted through massive inventory draws and forced demand loss, especially in the Middle East, Asia, and Africa, with Europe and potentially the US still needing to absorb more of the rebalancing. JPM’s key conclusion is that current prices have not yet done enough work to clear the market globally, implying further tightening and higher prices may be required.
important_observations: What looks like demand destruction is, in JPM's view, largely forced demand loss caused by missing physical supply rather than normal price elasticity.
The usual first shock absorber, Gulf spare capacity, is effectively unavailable, making this disruption structurally harder to offset than a typical supply outage.
The remaining balancing gap appears too large for EM demand weakness alone, implying Europe and the US may still need to absorb more rationing via higher prices.
Petchem feedstocks appear to be the earliest and largest point of adjustment, suggesting downstream industrial weakness could deepen before broader transport fuels fully roll over.
The Americas are relatively insulated near term, but JPM flags early signs that higher pump prices and airfares are beginning to dent US driving and jet demand.
new_data: JPM estimates global supply disruption at 9.1 mbd in March, widening to 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable commercial and strategic inventories drew 4.0 mbd in March and 7.1 mbd in April.
Observed global oil demand fell 2.8 mbd in March and is tracking down 4.3 mbd in April.
Middle East, Asia, and Africa account for 87% of the 4.3 mbd April demand hit.
Feedstock-driven weakness represents about 55% of April demand loss; jet fuel is about 11% of the loss.
Russian supply is said to be down 350 kbd in recent weeks, while Russia's spare capacity is only about 300 kbd.
US shale response is limited near term, with only 0.3-0.7 mbd likely over 3-6 months and up to 1 mbd over 6-12 months.
India March LPG consumption fell 13% YoY, cited as evidence of feedstock/fuel stress.
evidence_quality: Company-reported/public market data: draws on Kpler, IEA, EIA, JODI, Singapore, PAJ, Bloomberg, and Rystad; evidence base is largely market/industry data rather than company-specific disclosures.
Analyst model work: core conclusions rely on JPM's balance math and inferred global demand-loss estimates; a meaningful portion is analyst synthesis rather than directly observed data.
Primary vs inference: inventory data are partly observable, but JPM explicitly notes incomplete visibility, especially in product stocks, so true draws may differ materially.
Triangulated but not fully verifiable in real time: the note combines multiple datasets and market price signals, but several conclusions depend on interpreting shortages and residual balancing gaps.
No mgmt meeting/call, channel checks, or survey evidence is cited; weight should be placed on macro balance analysis, with caution around fast-moving disruption assumptions.
risks_or_watch_items: Further price upside may be needed if Europe and the US must participate more fully in demand rationing.
Inventory visibility is incomplete, especially for product stocks, creating risk that actual draws are either larger or less sustainable than observed.
Jet demand could weaken further in May as airlines across Asia and Europe cut activity.
Diesel and jet markets in Europe already appear tight; additional refinery constraints could broaden product stress into gasoline during the US summer driving season.
A slower-than-expected non-Gulf supply response, especially from US shale, would keep the market tighter for longer.
Regional dislocations in Asia and Africa could intensify if cargoes continue to be diverted toward higher-bidding markets.
key_numbers: Supply disruption: 9.1 mbd in March; 13.7 mbd in April.
Observable inventory draws: 4.0 mbd in March; 7.1 mbd in April.
Demand loss: 2.8 mbd in March; 4.3 mbd in April.
Residual balancing need: about 2 mbd even assuming an aggressive 8 mbd inventory contribution.
Regional concentration: 87% of April demand hit in Middle East, Asia, and Africa.
Feedstocks share of April demand loss: about 55%; jet fuel share: 11%.
Brent futures avg: just under $100/bbl in March and April to date; dated crude avg: $107 in March and $123 in April to date.
US shale response: 0.3-0.7 mbd over 3-6 months; up to 1.0 mbd over 6-12 months.
Russia spare capacity: about 300 kbd; recent Russian supply decline: 350 kbd.
India LPG consumption: down 13% YoY in March.

reports.title: Rise of the AI Agent - Global Implications
reports.headline: Agentic AI broadens the AI trade from GPUs to full-stack infra, with CPU orchestration and memory becoming the next major bottlenecks.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley argues agentic AI shifts value creation from standalone accelerators toward system orchestration, lifting CPU, DRAM, packaging, foundry and infra content across the stack. The note frames CPU as the control plane for multi-step AI workflows and estimates agentic workloads can add $32.5-60bn of incremental CPU TAM by 2030 within a $100bn+ server CPU market, alongside 15-45EB of incremental DRAM demand. The firm highlights global beneficiaries across memory, substrates, foundry, BMC/memory interface, storage and semi-cap, and raises PTs on key Korea names tied to memory and ABF exposure.
important_observations: The bottleneck is shifting from raw GPU throughput to orchestration, data movement and memory hierarchy, which broadens AI upside beyond accelerators.
CPU-side orchestration is framed as the dominant latency source in agentic workloads, implying structurally higher cluster CPU intensity and a rising CPU:GPU ratio.
Memory is presented as an active architectural layer rather than passive storage, supporting persistent context, recall and continuous learning.
Morgan Stanley prefers exposure via enablers where capex/token growth maps more directly into earnings upside, especially memory, foundry, ABF and infrastructure ICs.
Supply-constrained layers such as leading-edge foundry, ABF substrates, BMCs, memory interconnect and equipment are positioned to capture outsized economics as system complexity rises.
The note suggests agentic AI can extend the storage cycle as persistent context and knowledge layers increase HDD and NAND demand, not just DRAM.
new_data: Introduces a new Morgan Stanley framework to size CPU and memory upside from agentic AI.
Estimates incremental CPU TAM of $32.5-60bn by 2030.
Estimates total server CPU TAM of $100bn+ by 2030; executive summary cites $82.5-110bn.
Estimates 15-45EB of incremental DRAM demand by 2030, equal to 26-77% of 2027 annual DRAM supply.
States CPU-side orchestration can represent 50-90% of total workload latency.
Estimates agentic AI could drive 5-10% incremental ABF substrate demand upside by 2030 from enlarged CPU TAM alone.
Highlights a shift in server architecture from roughly 1 CPU:12 GPU historically toward 1:8, 1:2, and potentially 2:1 in newer platforms.
management_insights: Cites NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang at GTC 2026 saying the CPU is increasingly driving AI systems rather than merely supporting them.
Notes NVIDIA expects standalone CPUs to become a multi-billion-dollar business over time, with Meta and CoreWeave cited as early customers.
Says GUC mgmt indicated next-gen Arm-based CPU design wins with major customers including Google and Microsoft.
Frames industry positioning as increasingly shaped by hyperscaler demand for vertically integrated, AI-optimized compute platforms.

estimate_change_details.note: SK hynix PT raised.
Samsung Electronics common PT raised.
Samsung Electronics preferred PT raised.
Samsung Electro-Mechanics PT raised.

evidence_quality: Analyst model work: core TAM, latency and architecture conclusions are primarily Morgan Stanley estimates and framework-driven; useful for scenario analysis but not primary evidence.
Market/industry data: includes references to Mercury Research, IDC, Prismark and FactSet-style market/share data, which adds external support but still requires interpretation.
Management/public commentary: uses public statements and roadmaps from NVIDIA and others; relevant directional evidence, but not equivalent to company-reported financial guidance.
Channel checks: supply tightness, pricing power and capacity-constraint views are partly supported by channel checks; informative but inherently anecdotal.
Mostly triangulated and inference-heavy rather than company-reported results; weight should be moderate for thematic positioning, lower for precise sizing.
risks_or_watch_items: Actual CPU attach and CPU:GPU ratio progression in production agentic clusters may lag the report's framework.
Leading-edge foundry, ABF and memory supply constraints could limit volume realization even if demand materializes.
Some stock calls depend on outer-year capex and architecture shifts that remain partly roadmap-based rather than booked demand.
Competitive dynamics across NVIDIA, AMD, Arm and Intel may change faster than assumed as specialized AI CPUs evolve.
TCO and enterprise adoption curves for agentic workloads remain uncertain and could slow monetization of the projected TAM.
key_numbers: $32.5-60bn incremental CPU TAM by 2030
$100bn+ total server CPU TAM by 2030
15-45EB incremental DRAM demand by 2030
26-77% of 2027 DRAM supply from incremental agentic demand
50-90% of workload latency attributed to CPU-side orchestration
5-10% incremental ABF demand upside by 2030
Samsung Electronics PT: W251,000 -> W362,000
Samsung Electronics pref PT: W213,350 -> W289,600
Samsung Electro-Mechanics PT: W500,000 -> W710,000

reports.title: CY26 Data Center Capex Revised Higher and Strong Initial Growth Outlook of 40% for CY27; Positive Across the Semiconductor AI Value Chain - Potential for Continued Upward Revisions
reports.companies_covered: AMD, ADI, AMAT, ARM, ALAB, AVGO, CDNS, KLAC, LRCX, MTSI, MRVL, MU, NVDA, SNPS
reports.headline: JPM raises CY26 cloud/data center capex growth view and introduces strong CY27 outlook, reinforcing a bullish stance on the semiconductor AI value chain.
reports.summary: J.P. Morgan argues AI infrastructure spend remains in an upward revision cycle, lifting its data center capex growth outlook to 63% for CY26 from 52% and introducing +40% growth for CY27. The note frames sustained hyperscaler and model-lab capacity constraints, rising inference and reasoning workload intensity, and growing backlog visibility at NVDA, AVGO, and MU as evidence that AI compute demand should continue supporting broad upside across semis, custom ASIC vendors, memory, semicaps, and EDA.
important_observations: JPM sees another year of upward capex revisions forming in 2026, similar to the pattern seen through 2025, implying consensus may still be too low for AI infra spend.
Custom AI ASICs are expected to gain share vs GPUs in CY26/CY27, creating a relative mix tailwind for AVGO and MRVL while still leaving overall accelerator demand very strong.
The report argues backlog and order-book visibility across semi coverage already points to robust CY27 demand, not just a short-cycle 2026 spike.

Installed North America DC power capacity growth is presented as a hard-asset constraint/supporting datapoint behind the spend outlook, reducing the risk that the capex thesis is purely narrative-driven.
JPM expects coming qtrs to show constructive commentary on customer activity, bookings, backlog, and supply-chain capacity, which could serve as incremental positive catalysts for AI-linked names.
new_data: CY26 data center capex growth forecast raised to 63% from 52%.
Initial CY27 data center capex growth forecast introduced at 40%.
More than $200bn of incremental data center capex implied in 2026, a record annual increase.
North American planned data center power capacity tracked at 140GW+ at end-2025.
Installed DC capacity forecast: 48GW in 2025, 60GW+ in 2026, 75GW+ in 2027.
JPM cites NVDA discussing $1T+ of Blackwell and Vera Rubin demand visibility through CY27.
JPM cites AVGO discussing $100B+ in AI rev in FY27.
JPM cites MU signing the industry's first five-year CSA.
management_insights: NVDA and AVGO commentary suggests extended visibility into CY27 as customers lock in capacity well ahead of deployment.
Hyperscalers are portrayed as remaining capacity-constrained and committed to scaling AI infrastructure despite already elevated spending.
Mgmt commentary cited by JPM supports continued GPU and XPU ramps over the next few qtrs.
Recent AVGO customer commitment and partnership commentary is used to support growing custom ASIC adoption.
The note frames inference-led demand and reasoning/agentic workloads as key drivers of rising compute intensity.

estimate_change_details.note: JPM revised CY26 growth outlook higher.
Initial CY27 forecast; no prior figure provided.
Forecast level disclosed, but no prior estimate given.
Forecast level disclosed, but no prior estimate given.

evidence_quality: Primary plus analyst synthesis: cites company-reported/backlog commentary from NVDA, AVGO, and MU alongside JPM sector views.
Triangulated with external market/industry data: 650 Group and Datacenter Hawk used for capex and capacity framing.
Mostly forward-looking analyst model work for CY26/CY27 capex and capacity forecasts, so conclusions depend on sustained hyperscaler follow-through.
Evidence base is stronger on demand visibility and capacity build plans than on exact downstream monetization by each covered stock.
Some conclusions on GPU vs custom ASIC mix shift are partly analyst inference, though supported by recent company commentary.
risks_or_watch_items: If hyperscaler capex pacing slips or deployment timing pushes out, the 2026-27 upward revision thesis could moderate.
The bullish CY27 setup assumes limited inflation/commodity pressure; renewed cost inflation could alter spend efficiency and mix.
A faster mix shift toward custom ASICs could redistribute value within the AI stack even if total spend stays strong.
Backlog visibility may not fully translate into near-term shipments if supply-chain bottlenecks or power constraints emerge.
Watch upcoming commentary on bookings, backlog, supply capacity, and customer ramp timing for confirmation of continued estimate upside.
key_numbers: CY26 data center capex growth: 63% vs prior 52%
CY27 data center capex growth: 40%
Incremental 2026 data center capex: $200bn+
North America planned DC power capacity at end-2025: 140GW+
Installed DC capacity: 48GW in 2025, 60GW+ in 2026, 75GW+ in 2027
NVDA demand visibility through CY27: $1T+ for Blackwell and Vera Rubin
AVGO AI rev discussed for FY27: $100bn+

reports.title: Microsoft Fairwater Going Live (ANET Positive), Korea Memory Exports, DRAM & NAND Forecasts, iPhone Outperformance, & More
reports.companies_covered: ANET, AAPL, MSFT, MU, AMD, RMBS, CDNS, NTAP, ASML
reports.headline: Wells Fargo tech weekly argues AI infra and memory datapoints remain constructive, with ANET, AAPL, MU, RMBS, CDNS and ASML tied to improving demand signals and rising industry forecasts.
reports.summary: Wells Fargo’s weekly tech update highlights several constructive AI and memory datapoints: Microsoft’s Fairwater AI DC appears live ahead of schedule, which the analysts view as positive for ANET; South Korea March memory exports and TrendForce’s revised DRAM/NAND forecasts reinforce a stronger memory upcycle; preliminary 1Q26 smartphone data point to AAPL materially outperforming the handset market; and AI infra spending forecasts, LPDDR adoption, and Cadence’s AI-agent product rollout support a favorable backdrop for select networking, memory, EDA, and semi-cap names. The note is thematic and data-driven rather than a formal rating/PT change report.
important_observations: MSFT Fairwater going live ahead of schedule is framed as a real-world AI cluster deployment signal that could support ANET as MSFT leans harder into Ethernet networking.
Korea memory export growth and TrendForce forecast hikes both point to a sharper-than-expected memory upcycle, with ASP inflation doing most of the work.
AAPL appears to be taking share in a weak global smartphone market, with scale and memory procurement positioned as a strategic advantage in a constrained DRAM/NAND backdrop.
The note pushes a variant view that LPDDR becomes a large DC memory TAM, which matters positively for MU and creates a more nuanced debate around RMBS chipset content.
Cadence’s AI agent launches and deeper Google/NVIDIA ties suggest a possible path to monetization upside beyond its usual growth algorithm if usage-based pricing gains traction.
Anthropic compute constraints are presented as a potential competitive opening for OpenAI, implying AI compute availability may increasingly shape model quality and vendor positioning.
new_data: MSFT Fairwater Wisconsin AI DC reportedly went live ahead of schedule; site said to deploy hundreds of thousands of NVIDIA GB200 GPUs.
South Korea March DRAM exports were $8.0B, +176% y/y and +23% m/m; 1Q26 DRAM exports were +248% y/y and +84% q/q.
South Korea March NAND exports were $2.5B, +383% y/y and +81% m/m; 1Q26 NAND exports were +377% y/y and +77% q/q.
South Korea March total semiconductor exports reached $32.8B, +151% y/y, surpassing $30B for the first time.
Preliminary 1Q26 smartphone data from Gartner/IDC/Counterpoint show global smartphone shipments down ~4-6% y/y while AAPL iPhone shipments rose ~3-6% y/y and share reached ~19-21%.
AAPL purchase commitments rose to $33.2B exiting F1Q26 from $13.3B prior qtr; F2027-F2030+ commitments rose to $28.4B from $8.5B.
Apple MacBook Neo demand is described as stronger than expected, with 2026 shipment expectations revised to 10M from an initial 5-8M.
TrendForce raised 2026 DRAM revenue forecast to $594.2B from $501.8B and 2026 NAND revenue forecast to $225.9B from $217.8B.
AMD’s next-gen Verano EPYC is expected to support LPDDR5X SOCAMM2 alongside R-DIMM and MR-DIMM, with launch expected in 2H27.
Gartner increased long-term AI spending forecasts, with 2026 AI spend raised by $67.8B, mostly from AI infrastructure/server demand.
management_insights: Satya Nadella’s post indicated Fairwater is live ahead of schedule and one of several similar sites with identical architecture, implying broader MSFT buildout.
Wells says recent conversations with RMBS suggest its ~$600M incremental MR-DIMM TAM assumes only a low-single-digit attach rate on next-gen CPU platforms.

Cadence management commentary/product messaging emphasized usage-based monetization potential, autonomous AI coverage across chip design workflows, and 32x faster GPU-accelerated CFD versus CPU-only runs.
NetApp/Google messaging centered on sovereign and air-gapped deployments for gov’t/defense AI architectures, highlighting secure storage as part of AI infra buildout.

estimate_change_details.note: TrendForce monthly forecast revision; increase driven primarily by higher ASP assumptions.
TrendForce monthly forecast revision.
TrendForce monthly forecast revision.
Prior estimate given as a range, so pct change is not computed.

evidence_quality: Primary company/management sourcing: Satya Nadella post on Fairwater, AAPL disclosed purchase commitments, CadenceLIVE and NetApp/Google announcements.
Primary market/industry data: South Korea export data, China customs smartphone export data, Gartner/IDC/Counterpoint shipment reads, TrendForce and Gartner forecast updates.
Triangulated but mixed-quality evidence: note combines company-reported data with third-party industry trackers and supply-chain/media reports such as DigiTimes, 9to5Mac, SemiAnalysis, and Wccftech.
Some conclusions are analyst inference rather than hard company guidance, especially around ANET benefit, MU/RMBS implications, and AI model competitive positioning.
risks_or_watch_items: Need confirmation that Fairwater deployment translates into incremental ANET orders rather than timing noise within existing MSFT spend.
Memory forecasts are increasingly ASP-driven; any easing in price inflation could compress the bullish DRAM/NAND setup.
AAPL outperformance thesis depends partly on memory supply lock-in and pricing strategy; China demand and supply-chain diversification remain moving parts.
LPDDR adoption in servers could create content shifts that complicate the RMBS thesis if SOCAMM2 scales faster than expected.
Cadence AI-agent monetization is still early; key watch items are pricing model, contract uplift, and durability of adoption beyond product launch optics.
Anthropic/OpenAI commentary is partly anecdotal and should be treated as lower-confidence competitive color rather than a core investment datapoint.
key_numbers: MSFT accounted for ~26% of ANET 2025 rev.
Fairwater described as a $7B+ DC.
March DRAM exports: $8.0B, +176% y/y, +23% m/m.
March NAND exports: $2.5B, +383% y/y, +81% m/m.
1Q26 DRAM exports: +248% y/y, +84% q/q.
1Q26 NAND exports: +377% y/y, +77% q/q.
South Korea March semiconductor exports: $32.8B, +151% y/y.
AAPL iPhone 1Q26 share: ~19-21% vs. ~18-20% prior year.
AAPL purchase commitments: $33.2B vs. $13.3B prior qtr.
TrendForce 2026 DRAM forecast: $594.2B vs. $501.8B prior.
TrendForce 2026 NAND forecast: $225.9B vs. $217.8B prior.
Gartner 2026 AI spend raised by $67.8B; AI infra/server spend drove $65B of the increase.
Cadence says Blackwell-accelerated CFD can run 32x faster than CPU-only.
ASML weekly buyback: €98M; QTD: €198M; remaining authorization est. €10.75B.

reports.title: New OpenAI Agreement Clarifies Relationship – Cleaner Path Ahead for MSFT
reports.headline: Barclays views the revised OpenAI agreement as a net positive for MSFT because it clarifies economics, removes AGI-related complexity, preserves Azure's product-first position, and reduces MSFT's capital burden from OpenAI infrastructure needs.
reports.summary: Barclays argues the new OpenAI agreement creates a cleaner and more workable structure for both parties and is a net positive for MSFT. The note highlights that MSFT retains revenue share through 2030 regardless of AGI progress, keeps OpenAI IP rights through 2032 on a non-exclusive basis, and benefits from Azure remaining the first cloud for new OpenAI innovation. Barclays also sees strategic value in MSFT no longer needing to fund all of OpenAI's data center buildout, freeing capital for Copilot and broader cloud/GPU capacity while allowing both firms to pursue more independent enterprise AI strategies.
important_observations: The revised structure appears to reduce strategic friction as OpenAI expands across multiple clouds while MSFT develops its own LLM and enterprise AI stack.
Removal of the AGI clause materially simplifies the commercial relationship and lowers the risk that economics would be disrupted by a subjective AGI trigger.
Azure keeping first access to new OpenAI innovation preserves a product and go-to-market edge even as exclusivity eases.
Reduced obligation to support all OpenAI data center demand could improve MSFT capital allocation flexibility toward Copilot and core Azure/GPU capacity.
Barclays frames the agreement as enabling a cleaner separation just as OpenAI's enterprise ambitions increasingly overlap with MSFT's own offerings.
new_data: MSFT receives OpenAI-related revenue share until 2030 independent of OpenAI technology progress.
MSFT maintains OpenAI IP rights until 2032, though no longer on an exclusive basis.
Azure remains the hyperscaler where new OpenAI innovation will be shipped first.
MSFT no longer shares its own revenue with OpenAI, according to Barclays' read of the new structure.
Barclays notes prior amendments to the OpenAI agreement on 2025-10-28 and 2026-02-27 before this latest change.

estimate_change_details.note: Current PT remains $600; no PT revision in this note.

evidence_quality: Primary source: company/blog announcement on the revised OpenAI agreement; useful for contract structure but limited on financial detail.
Mostly analyst inference: positive read on capital allocation, competitive positioning, and enterprise AI implications is Barclays interpretation rather than company-quantified guidance.
Some historical context: note references prior amendments in October 2025 and February 2026, which helps triangulate that this is part of an ongoing restructuring.
Limited hard financial evidence: no detailed company-reported rev, margin, or EPS impact disclosed in the note, so investment impact is directional rather than precisely quantified.
risks_or_watch_items: Whether OpenAI's multi-cloud strategy reduces Azure's practical advantage over time despite first-ship status.
How much retained but non-exclusive IP access through 2032 translates into durable product differentiation for MSFT.
Potential competitive overlap as OpenAI pushes further into enterprise products and collides more directly with MSFT offerings.
Whether reduced OpenAI infrastructure obligations actually improve MSFT capital intensity or simply reallocate spend toward Copilot and GPU demand.
Execution risk around MSFT's internal LLM strategy versus reliance on OpenAI innovation.
key_numbers: Rating: Overweight
Industry View: Positive
Implied upside/downside: +41.3%
OpenAI-related revenue share retained until 2030
OpenAI IP rights retained until 2032
Barclays PT methodology: ~30x CY27E EPS of $20.23

reports.title: Initiate at Buy: Dash to Grow Share of Global Food & Retail Commerce; $225 PT
reports.headline: ['Initiation at Buy with $225 PT on view that DASH can keep taking share in food delivery and broader retail commerce while expanding margins.']
reports.summary: TD Cowen initiates DASH at Buy with a $225 PT, arguing US delivery leadership, intl expansion via Wolt/Deliveroo, Grocery & Retail penetration, and higher-margin ads/commerce tools can support sustained rev growth and rising profitability. The note leans on proprietary NA share work and a US consumer survey to support a thesis of steady MAU/order-frequency gains, while acknowledging competitive and regulatory risks and noting TD's 2026 rev/EBITDA ests sit modestly below Street.
important_observations: Core debate is whether elevated 2026 tech/replatforming spend is dilutive or high-ROI; TD argues it sets up accelerating 2027 EPS and possible multiple expansion.
Grocery & Retail is framed as the key incremental demand vector because it should lift both order frequency and basket size while broadening merchant relevance beyond restaurants.
Ads and commerce tools are positioned as the main margin lever, with ad monetization expected to scale materially as a % of GOV.
Intl mix shift is increasingly important: rev mix from intl is projected to rise materially as Wolt/Deliveroo are integrated onto a unified AI-native platform.
Variant view is that agentic commerce/LLM interfaces are a tailwind rather than a disintermediating threat because DASH keeps the hard logistics layer and can benefit from added demand.
new_data: Proprietary NA delivery share model projects DASH share rising to 52.6% in 2028 from 50.9% in 2025.
US survey of ~2,500 respondents shows 26% used DASH in the last 30 days in 4Q25 vs 18% for Uber Eats.
Survey indicates 63% of DASH MAUs are not also Uber Eats MAUs; Uber users show higher cross-platform overlap.
Survey shows 40% of DASH MAUs are DashPass members in 4Q25; over 83% of DASH MAUs are also Amazon Prime members.
Online grocery penetration in the survey returned to 36% in 4Q25, back to pandemic-peak levels.
TD estimates ad rev at $1.6B in 2025, growing to $9.5B by 2030.
TD estimates Grocery & Retail GOV at $8.1B in 2025, or 8% of total GOV, with 25% CAGR to 2030.
management_insights: Mgmt expects the new tech platform shipping in 2026 to improve developer productivity and product velocity across regions.
Mgmt has said the intl business was close to contribution profit breakeven as of 3Q25.
Mgmt has indicated most first-time Grocery & Retail orders come from existing DASH users, supporting incremental order frequency.
Mgmt expects Grocery & Retail unit economics to turn positive in 2H26.
Mgmt cited 25%+ of MAUs shopping Grocery & Retail in 2Q25 and 35M+ global subscribers exiting 2025.
Internal company data cited by TD says SmartScale reduced missing-item claims by up to 30%.

estimate_change_details.note: Initiation model; no prior TD estimate disclosed. About 1% below consensus of $17,845M.
Initiation model; no prior TD estimate disclosed. Slightly below consensus of $21,372M.
Initiation model; no prior TD estimate disclosed. About 1% below consensus of $3,605M.
Initiation model; no prior TD estimate disclosed. Slightly below consensus of $4,792M.
Coverage initiated at $225 PT; no prior TD PT because this is an initiation.

evidence_quality: Primary/company-reported: uses DASH reported operating metrics, subscriber figures, historical financials, and mgmt commentary from calls/shareholder materials.
Proprietary dataset: TD NA market-share model and a ~2,500-person US consumer survey add differentiated evidence, but survey/channel-style work is still indirect.
Analyst model work: long-term 2025-2031 MAU, GOV, ad rev, margin, and EPS/CAGR views are largely forecast-driven and should be weighted as inference.
Triangulated but mixed: combines company data, FactSet consensus, industry data, and survey work; stronger on directional adoption/share trends than on exact long-term monetization outcomes.
risks_or_watch_items: AMZN is the clearest strategic risk given 83%+ Prime overlap, faster same-day delivery expansion, and broader online grocery push.
Uber Eats, Instacart, and a potentially revived GrubHub/Wonder can pressure share and keep promo intensity elevated.
Regulatory risk remains around independent-contractor classification and wage rules.
Execution risk on tech-stack unification/replatforming across DoorDash, Wolt, and Deliveroo could delay expected efficiency gains.
Agentic/LLM checkout could pressure economics if third-party AI interfaces capture take rates.
Autonomous delivery entrants could become both partners and competitors over time.
key_numbers: Rating/PT: Buy, $225 PT vs $176.78 close on 2026-04-24.
2026E Adj. EBITDA: $3.56B; 2027E Adj. EBITDA: $4.71B.
TD forecasts 2025-2030 rev CAGR of 18% pro forma and Adj. EBITDA CAGR of 27%.
TD cites 20% rev CAGR and 43% EPS CAGR from 2025-2030 in the bull framing.
MAUs: 56M+ currently; TD sees 35M members in 2025 rising to 46.5M by 2028.
NA share forecast: 50.9% in 2025 to 52.6% in 2028.
Intl rev mix forecast: 23% of 4Q25 rev to 37% by 2030.
Ad rev forecast: $1.6B in 2025 to $9.5B in 2030; ad rev/GOV from 1.6% to 3.9%.
Grocery & Retail GOV forecast: $8.1B in 2025, 8% of total GOV.

reports.title: F3Q26 Preview: Move CY26 & CY27 EPS Ests to ~$125 and ~$150; PT to $975 (See 6-7x P/E on Peak as Still Appropriate)
reports.headline: Wells lifts SNDK sharply into F3Q26 on stronger NAND pricing, AI/eSSD demand, and higher peak EPS assumptions, but stays EW on valuation.
reports.summary: Wells Fargo sharply raises SNDK ests and PT ahead of F3Q26, arguing it had underappreciated EPS leverage from NAND price inflation and a more disciplined supply backdrop through 2027. The note pushes CY26/CY27 EPS to roughly $125/$150 in the narrative and materially above prior published ests in the model, with the bull case increasingly tied to AI-driven eSSD/KV-cache demand, LTAs, and structurally higher GM. Even so, Wells remains EW because it views the stock as already discounting peak earnings at roughly 6-7x peak EPS, while the key debate shifts toward durability of thru-cycle margins and whether SNDK can convert AI storage demand into sustained eSSD share gains.
important_observations: The central debate has shifted from near-term upside to how much of current earnings power is peak versus sustainable thru-cycle profitability.
Analyst now views NAND vendor supply discipline through 2027 as more credible, supporting a materially stronger pricing and margin framework than previously assumed.
Buy-side focus appears to be moving toward +80% GM and $150+/sh EPS scenarios, versus company-guided 65%-67% GM for F3Q26.
eSSD share remains the main strategic swing factor: low current share is being interpreted by some investors as upside in a supply-constrained market rather than a weakness.
KV-cache/inference storage demand is increasingly treated as a legitimate NAND demand driver, with Wells supportive of SNDK's 2027-2028 opportunity sizing.
Despite large est increases, the analyst still sees upside as capped by a consensus valuation framework anchored to 6-7x peak EPS.
new_data: PT raised to $975 from $675.
F3Q26 est raised to $5.029B rev and $16.59 non-GAAP EPS from prior $4.60B and $14.24.
FY26 est raised to $17.95B rev and $54.05 EPS from $15.10B and $38.53.
FY27 est raised to $37.47B rev and $151.65 EPS from $22.73B and $73.58.
Wells' FY27 EPS is 53.9% above consensus; FY26 EPS is 38.8% above consensus.
Model now assumes peak GM near 82% in FY27 and 81% in FY28.
management_insights: Mgmt guided F3Q26 to roughly $4.6B rev and $13.00 EPS at the midpoint.
Mgmt guided F3Q26 GM to 65%-67%.
Mgmt has kept capex discipline and mid/high-teens bit growth targets unchanged since the Feb. 25 investor day.
Mgmt previously noted a 2nd hyperscaler qualification for Gen5 TLC eSSD, with additional quals expected.
Mgmt said QLC-based Stargate eSSDs are progressing through two hyperscaler qualifications, with rev expected in coming qtrs.
Mgmt raised its CY26 eSSD bit-demand view to high-60% growth and disclosed one signed LTA with a prepayment component, with several more discussions underway.
Mgmt has said HBF samples should ship in 2H26, with AI processors using HBF targeted for 2027.

estimate_change_details.note: PT increased while rating remains Equal Weight.
Raised vs prior analyst est; above company guide midpoint of $4.6B.
Raised vs prior analyst est; above company guide midpoint of $13.00.
Uses published summary-table values.
Published notable-change table; narrative cites an even more aggressive CY26 view of roughly $125 EPS on a calendar-year basis.
Uses published summary-table values.
Published notable-change table; narrative cites roughly $150 CY27 EPS on a calendar-year basis.

evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported results/guidance and prior mgmt commentary form the core near-term basis; high weight for F3Q26 guide, GM, data-center rev, and product qualification updates.
Triangulated industry data: TrendForce pricing/industry forecasts, South Korea export data, and Taiwanese supplier sales support the pricing/demand thesis; useful but still external/market-level.
Analyst model work: the large EPS/rev revisions and peak-margin framework are mostly Wells inference built on NAND pricing and GM leverage assumptions; high sensitivity to pricing persistence.
Anecdotal/channel-style inputs: investor discussions, buy-side sentiment, and LTA conversation framing inform the debate but are not hard evidence of durable structural change.
The KV-cache demand sizing is partly supported by third-party technical references and Wells math, but remains forward-looking and less proven than company-reported demand.
risks_or_watch_items: Whether NAND spot-price deceleration starts to pull contract pricing lower or compress duration of peak margins.
Durability of LTAs and whether they represent true structural change versus cyclical tightness.
Execution on eSSD share gains, especially Gen5 TLC and Stargate QLC ramps with hyperscalers.
How investors settle on thru-cycle GM and EPS once peak pricing normalizes.
Risk that AI/KV-cache demand is overstated if efficiency gains, compression, or architectural changes reduce storage needs.
HBF remains a 2027+ option with technical/adoption risk and limited near-term contribution.
key_numbers: Price $920.99 as of 2026-04-17; PT $975; implied upside 5.9%.
Rating: Equal Weight.
FY26E: rev $18.0B, EPS $54.05, EBIT $10.0B, EBITDA $10.2B.
FY27E: rev $37.5B, EPS $151.65, EBIT $28.5B, EBITDA $28.6B.
F2Q26 data-center rev was $440M, 14.5% of total rev, up 64% q/q.
Wells vs consensus EPS: FY26 +38.8%, FY27 +53.9%.
Base-case valuation is ~6-7x CY27 EPS; downside case $600, upside case $1,150.
Upcoming catalyst: F3Q26 earnings on 2026-04-30.

reports.title: 1Q26 Model Update: More Challenging Path Ahead
reports.headline: Barclays says HCA faces a tougher path to 6-9% 2026 core growth after 1Q26 headwinds and softer underlying mix/volume trends, cutting ests and lowering PT to $496.
reports.summary: Barclays remains Overweight on HCA but argues the path to management's 2026 core EBITDA growth target of 6-9% is getting harder after 1Q26 showed both transient disruption and softer underlying trends. The note highlights weaker employer-sponsored mix, fewer Medicaid conversions leading to higher uninsured volumes, and softer consumer confidence, while acknowledging upside from pending Medicaid state-directed payment programs. Barclays trimmed 2026-27 ests, cut its valuation multiple, and lowered PT to $496 from $551.
important_observations: Implied 1Q26 organic EBITDA growth was only ~3-4% depending on assumed resiliency savings, below the pace needed for the year.
Payor mix deterioration, not just weather/flu, is the more important debate point because it suggests a fundamental trend slowdown.
Potential upside from Florida and Nevada Medicaid SDPs is sizable at $300-600mn+, but Barclays says much of that already appears embedded in buy-side expectations.
To meet Street ests, HCA likely needs about $400mn of resiliency cost savings, manageable ACA exchange headwinds, and a meaningful core earnings reacceleration.
Barclays cut its valuation multiple to 9.5x from 10.25x, reflecting more uncertainty around commercial trends and macro pressure from softer consumer confidence and inflation/geopolitics.
new_data: 2026E EBITDA bridge now points to $15,938mn with 5-6% organic growth assumptions.
2027E EBITDA bridge points to $16,856mn with ~5% organic growth.
Pending Medicaid SDP upside estimated at $300-600mn+ across Florida and Nevada.
1Q26 included a $180mn flu/weather headwind in Barclays' bridge.
Barclays models ACA enhanced subsidy headwinds of $750mn in 2026 and $150mn in 2027.
Same-store adjusted admissions growth slowed to 1.3% in Mar-26 from 2.5% in Dec-25.
Same-store revenue per adjusted admission slowed to 3.1% in Mar-26 from 2.9% in Dec-25 and well below 2024 peaks.
management_insights: 2026 core growth guidance remains 6-9%, but Barclays views delivery as increasingly challenging after 1Q26.
1Q26 headwinds were described as partly transient from flu/weather.
Underlying trends weakened due to slightly worse employer-sponsored payor mix.
Fewer Medicaid conversions in the immigrant population drove higher uninsureds.

estimate_change_details.note: Barclays lowered FY2026 EPS modestly; rounded whole-percent change from old to new is 0%.
Barclays lowered FY2027 EPS estimate.
Barclays said FY2026 EBITDA estimate was reduced by 1%, but the prior absolute value was not explicitly shown.
Barclays said FY2027 EBITDA estimate was reduced by 1%, but the prior absolute value was not explicitly shown.
PT lowered as Barclays cut its valuation multiple to 9.5x from 10.25x on 2027 EBITDA-NCI.

evidence_quality: Primary: grounded in company-reported 1Q26 results/guidance and disclosed operating trends; high relevance for near-term outlook.
Primary plus analyst model work: EBITDA bridges, EPS/EBITDA est revisions, and PT are Barclays model outputs rather than company guidance.
Partly triangulated/inferential: commentary on buy-side expectations, commercial trend uncertainty, and required resiliency savings reflects analyst judgment.
Market/industry data: stock price, consensus, and valuation context come from Bloomberg/Barclays; useful but secondary to company-reported datapoints.
risks_or_watch_items: Watch whether employer-sponsored mix and uninsured trends improve or continue to pressure core growth.
Track realization of resiliency program savings, especially whether HCA can deliver roughly $400mn.
Monitor Florida and Nevada Medicaid SDP timing, grandfathering status, and program adjustments.
ACA enhanced subsidy headwinds remain material in Barclays' model and could be worse or better than expected.
Soft consumer confidence could weigh on commercial utilization and mix.
Labor, supplies, and other operating cost trends remain key downside risks if inflation re-accelerates.
key_numbers: Rating: Overweight unchanged
PT: $496, down from $551
Implied upside/downside: +14.7%
2026E adj EBITDA: $15,938mn
2027E adj EBITDA: $16,856mn
2026E adj EPS: $30.26 vs prior $30.33
2027E adj EPS: $34.08 vs prior $34.53
Implied 1Q26 organic growth: ~3-4%
Potential Medicaid SDP upside: $300-600mn+
2026 flu/weather headwind in bridge: $180mn
2026 resiliency initiatives in bridge: $400mn
Valuation multiple: 9.5x 2027 EBITDA-NCI vs prior 10.25x

reports.title: 1Q Preview: Margin Execution, Emerging Platform Optionality?
reports.headline: MS reiterates OW and $450 PT on CVNA ahead of 1Q26, arguing near-term volatility should be used opportunistically if retail GPU holds and mgmt supports a recovery path.
reports.summary: Morgan Stanley previews CVNA's 1Q26 print with a constructive stance, reiterating OW and $450 PT while arguing investor caution is largely about near-term margin execution, macro sensitivity, and retail GPU recovery rather than a broken long-term thesis. The note frames the key debate around whether CVNA can sustain growth and margin progress through temporary reconditioning inefficiencies and fuel/logistics pressure, while also highlighting underappreciated optionality from the company's expanding new-car dealership strategy and longer-term platform uses in parts/service and autonomous fleet management.
important_observations: Investor positioning appears tactically cautious despite long-term bullishness, creating room for upside if 1Q retail GPU and forward commentary are merely solid rather than spectacular.
MS sees the current retail GPU pressure as transitory execution friction from reconditioning buildout, not a structural deterioration in unit economics.
Recent web data suggested unit deceleration exiting the qtr, but channel checks indicated a rebound in recent weeks; that tension is central to the setup into earnings.

The note argues the market may be underappreciating CVNA's emerging platform optionality beyond used autos, especially via new-car dealership acquisitions, parts/service adjacency, and longer-term fleet-management use cases.
MS expects less prescriptive 2Q/FY26 guidance this qtr given macro uncertainty, which could matter for sentiment even if core results are acceptable.
Capital intensity is flagged as a future debate: today's physical model looks unusually asset-light, but recon capacity expansion could require higher capex over time.
new_data: MS says reports indicate CVNA has acquired its 7th Stellantis new-car dealership, including 4 acquisitions in 2025 and 3 more in 2026.
Buy-side 1Q26 bogey is ~185k retail units, ~$3,100 non-GAAP retail GPU, and ~$635m-$650m Adj EBITDA.
MS 1Q26 forecast: 184,779 retail units, $5,929m rev, and $640m Adj EBITDA.
MS 2026 forecast: 815,808 retail units, $27,733m rev, and $3,140m Adj EBITDA vs consensus 768,417 units, $26,783m rev, and $2,911m Adj EBITDA.
Subprime auto delinquencies are cited as falling to 5.54% in March from 6.37% in January.
MS forecasts a 1 ppt compression in FY26 gain-on-sale margin to reflect near-term volatility.
management_insights: At MS's TMT conference, CEO Ernie Garcia said reconditioning cost pressures are transitory rather than structural.
Mgmt attributed recent cost pressure to less efficient IRC sites, winter weather, inventory build, and holiday season effects.
Mgmt highlighted examples of operational decisions that can be automated as scale increases, supporting the margin recovery thesis.
The note implies mgmt commentary on GPU recovery will be critical to restoring investor confidence in margins.
evidence_quality: Primary company/management sourcing: draws on prior company commentary and CEO remarks at Morgan Stanley's TMT conference; useful for understanding mgmt framing but not new reported results.
Market/industry data: uses consensus, FactSet/Visible Alpha, ABS spread data, and delinquency statistics; relatively solid for framing expectations and financing conditions.
Channel checks / web-research data: references third-party web traffic and checks on unit trends; informative but more anecdotal and lower-confidence than reported KPIs.
Analyst model work: key conclusions on EBITDA, valuation, PT, and optionality are substantially analyst inference layered on top of primary and market data.
Triangulated overall, but much of the near-term call depends on pre-earnings setup analysis rather than fresh company-reported results.
risks_or_watch_items: Retail GPU print and commentary on the timing of recovery from reconditioning inefficiencies.
Fuel-cost persistence and resulting pressure on inbound/outbound logistics, GPU, and EBIT.
Whether mgmt withholds detailed 2Q/FY26 guidance, which could weigh on sentiment.
Consumer credit performance and ABS spread direction, especially for lower-income borrowers if oil stress persists.
Potential future step-up in capex as CVNA moves toward expanding recon capacity beyond current ADESA-enabled footprint.
Execution and strategic clarity around the new-car dealership push and adjacent platform opportunities.
key_numbers: Rating/PT: Overweight, $450 PT; bull case $745; bear case $130.
Share price / market cap: $403.02 close on Apr 23, 2026; market cap $90.3bn.
1Q26 buy-side bogey: ~185k retail units, ~$3,100 retail GPU, ~$635m-$650m Adj EBITDA.
MSe 1Q26 vs cons: 184,779 vs 179,357 retail units; $640m vs $646m Adj EBITDA; $5,929m vs $6,083m rev.
MSe 2026 vs cons: 815,808 vs 768,417 retail units; $3,140m vs $2,911m Adj EBITDA; $27,733m vs $26,783m rev.
MS FY27 Adj EBITDA framing: ~$4.3bn in PT discussion vs ~$3.8bn consensus cited in valuation setup.
Reconditioning headwind: $220 per unit cost headwind in 4Q25 from less efficient IRC sites.
Logistics expense: ~7% of SG&A.
Credit datapoint: subprime delinquencies fell to 5.54% in March from 6.37% in January.
Valuation framing: PT implies roughly 24x-25x FY27 Adj EBITDA.

reports.title: F3Q Preview - Capacity Considerations
reports.headline: DB previews MSFT F3Q with likely modest rev upside and Azure strength, but argues AI capacity allocation to apps/security/internal R&D may cap near-term Azure upside and keep focus on proving AI monetization ex-Azure.
reports.summary: Deutsche Bank previews MSFT F3Q ahead of the April 29, 2026 print, expecting another solid qtr with rev around guidance-to-slightly above, Azure likely above the 37-38% cc guide but only modestly because constrained capacity is being allocated across Azure, first-party apps, security, and internal R&D. DB stays constructive on the long-term setup and keeps Buy/$575 PT, but argues the stock increasingly needs tangible AI monetization outside Azure as capex, allocation tradeoffs, and sentiment around competitive AI risk remain key debates.
important_observations: DB sees underlying Azure demand/supply support for growth above guide, but thinks allocation choices likely limit the beat to roughly 1 pt rather than a major upside print.

The central debate is not demand but where scarce AI capacity is deployed; prioritizing first-party apps, security, and internal R&D may sacrifice near-term Azure monetization for longer-term platform value.
DB estimates Azure contributes more than 60% of FY26 incremental rev, reinforcing that Azure remains the primary growth engine even as investors look for broader AI monetization.
The note argues investor burden is shifting toward evidence of AI payoff above the infra layer, especially in M365/Copilot and other application surfaces.
DB views fears of near-term competitive displacement in enterprise productivity/systems of record as overhyped, but acknowledges sentiment risk until usage and monetization data improve.
Commercial bookings could be stronger than expected due to renewals, policy changes around annual consumption thresholds, and customers reserving capacity for rising inference needs.
DB expects capex growth to decelerate materially in FY27 even if absolute spend remains up y/y, implying capacity constraints and allocation tradeoffs may persist beyond this qtr.
new_data: DB models F3Q rev/EPS at $81.2bn/$4.06 vs Street $81.3bn/$4.05.
DB models F4Q rev/EPS at $88.0bn/$4.27 vs Street $88.0bn/$4.25.
The note highlights 15m paid M365 Copilot seats as still early penetration, roughly ~1% of the broader M365 base by DB's framing.
DB cites prior mgmt commentary implying Azure could have grown above 40% if all incremental GPUs had been allocated there, suggesting $2bn+ ARR worth of capacity may be directed elsewhere.
The note points to announced M365 price increases effective July 1, 2026, including Office 365 E3 to $26 from $23 and Microsoft 365 E3 to $39 from $36.
DB cites recent product/strategy items including Agent 365 GA and a new E7 SKU starting May 1 as potential future ARPU tailwinds.
DB references commercial cRPO growth accelerating to 39% y/y and commercial RPO reaching $625bn as evidence of strong forward demand.
management_insights: Mgmt has indicated a relative preference to allocate AI capacity toward first-party apps, security features, and internal R&D rather than maximizing near-term Azure growth.
Last qtr mgmt suggested Azure growth could have exceeded 40% with different GPU allocation, implying deliberate tradeoffs rather than pure supply shortfall.
Mgmt last disclosed 15m paid M365 Copilot seats, supporting traction but still not enough to visibly change M365 Commercial Cloud growth yet.
Prior commentary framed Azure guidance as capacity-driven, and DB believes that characterization likely still holds for F3Q and likely F4Q.
Mgmt has guided F3Q commercial bookings to healthy core growth ex-OpenAI, supported by strong renewal rates.
DB expects mgmt to remain disciplined on op margin despite elevated AI investment and increased R&D focus.
The report flags org changes during F3Q including a unified Copilot organization across commercial and consumer plus a reorganized Copilot leadership team.
evidence_quality: Primary: company-reported historical results, guidance, product disclosures, and prior mgmt commentary form the core evidence base.
Analyst model work: the note is largely a preview built on DB segment forecasts, margin assumptions, and scenario analysis around capacity allocation.
Triangulated: DB supplements company data with IDC/Gartner PC shipment data, Bloomberg consensus, and industry capex/market datapoints.
Channel-check / anecdotal elements are present in bookings, migration, GPU pricing, and server demand commentary, so some conclusions are inferential rather than fully verified.
Overall weight: moderately strong for near-term setup and framing, but several key calls on Azure upside limits and AI monetization timing are still mostly analyst inference.
risks_or_watch_items: Azure upside may remain capped if incremental capacity continues to be diverted to apps, security, and internal AI R&D.
The stock may need clearer proof of AI monetization outside Azure, especially in M365/Copilot, to sustain sentiment.
Capex growth is still very high in FY26, while FY27 may bring slower growth and continued allocation friction.
Memory pricing, component costs, and geopolitical conflict could pressure capex efficiency, supply, and F4Q/FY27 planning.
Commercial bookings strength could still be constrained medium-term if customers want more capacity than MSFT can provision.
MPC remains volatile given PC demand, inventory normalization, Windows refresh timing, and gaming execution.
Potential moves into frontier models could increase compute intensity and crowd out externally monetizable workloads.
key_numbers: Rating/PT: Buy, $575 PT.
F3Q26E total rev $81.2bn, non-GAAP EPS $4.06, op income $36.6bn.
F3Q26 Azure guide: +37-38% y/y cc; DB bogey: 38-39%.
F4Q26E total rev $88.0bn, EPS $4.27.
F3Q26 capex est.: DB $34.5bn vs Street ~$35bn; F2Q actual capex incl. leases was $37.5bn.
FY26 capex growth expected above prior year; DB expects FY27 capex still up y/y but at a meaningfully lower growth rate than ~60% in FY26.
15m paid M365 Copilot seats disclosed last qtr; seat adds +160% y/y.
Commercial RPO: $625bn as of F2Q26; commercial cRPO growth: +39% y/y last qtr.
DB says current $575 PT implies 28x CY27E EPS versus Mag 7 median ~27x.
